retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Function -- Asthma Management in the Age Group 0 to 4 years
retrieved_docs.content: Step 1: The preferred therapy is PRN SABA (short-acting β2-agonist). In patients with recurrent wheezing, a short (7 to 10 days) course of ICS with PRN SABA is recommended at the onset of respiratory tract infection. The strategy of initiating ICS and PRN SABA decreases asthma exacerbations and reduces the use of systemic corticosteroids. Step 2: Daily low-dose ICS (inhaled corticosteroids) as controller therapy and PRN SABA as quick relief therapy. An alternative regimen is daily montelukast or cromolyn and PRN SABA. Step 3: Daily medium dose ICS as controller therapy and PRN SABA for quick relief therapy. Step 4: Daily medium dose ICS-LABA (long-acting β2-agonist) as controller therapy and PRN SABA for quick relief therapy. The alternative regimen is a daily medium dose of ICS + montelukast and PRN SABA. Step 5: Daily high-dose ICS-LABA as controller therapy and PRN SABA for quick relief therapy. The alternative regimen is a daily high dose of ICS + montelukast and PRN SABA.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Function -- Asthma Management in the Age Group 5 to 11 years
retrieved_docs.content: Step 1: PRN SABA Step 2: Daily low-dose ICS as controller therapy and PRN SABA as quick relief therapy. Daily therapy with LTRA, cromolyn, nedocromil, theophylline, and PRN SABA are alternative regimens. Step 3: Daily combination of low-dose ICS-formoterol as controller and quick relief therapy. The alternative regimen is daily medium-dose ICS and PRN SABA. Another alternative regimen is daily low-dose ICS-LABA, daily low-dose ICS + LTRA, or daily low-dose ICS + theophylline and PRN SABA. Step 4: Daily combination of medium-dose ICS-formoterol as controller therapy and medium-dose ICS-formoterol PRN for quick relief therapy. The alternative regimen is a daily medium dose of ICS-LABA as controller therapy and PRN SABA as quick relief therapy. Another alternative regimen is daily medium dose ICS + LTRA or daily medium dose ICS + theophylline as controller therapy and PRN SABA as quick relief therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Evaluation and Treatment of Asthma in Children -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: The evidence supporting systemic corticosteroid use by EMS providers is less substantial compared to that within the emergency department. Administration of steroids by prehospital personnel or EMS, irrespective of asthma severity, has been linked to reduced length of stay in the emergency department and rate of inpatient admissions, improved patient outcomes, and reduced ICU admissions. [38] The availability of oral corticosteroids in the prehospital setting is associated with a higher likelihood of administration in Houston, Texas.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone is superior to budesonide for improving symptom scores, but does not appear to reduce readmission rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Function -- Asthma Management for Ages >12 years
retrieved_docs.content: Step 1: PRN SABA Step 2: Daily low-dose ICS and PRN SABA or PRN concomitant ICS and SABA. An alternative regimen is daily LTRA and PRN SABA. An additional alternative regimen is cromolyn or nedocromil or zileuton or theophylline and PRN SABA (less preferred for use in the United States due to potential adverse drug reactions). Step 3: Daily and PRN combination low-dose ICS-formoterol. The alternative strategy is daily medium-dose ICS and PRN SABA. An alternative regimen is daily low-dose ICS + (LABA/LAMA/LTRA) and PRN SABA. LAMA is a long-acting muscarinic antagonist like tiotropium. [34] The optional regimen is daily low-dose ICS + theophylline or zileuton and PRN SABA (less preferred for use in the US due to potential adverse drug reactions).

retrieved_docs.title: Cromolyn Sodium -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate cromolyn sodium from other asthma and allergy treatments, understanding its unique mechanism as a mast cell stabilizer. Screen for potential allergic or adverse reactions associated with cromolyn sodium to ensure safe and effective treatment. Implement evidence-based guidelines for the prophylactic use of cromolyn sodium in patients with asthma and mast cell disorders. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes in patients treated with cromolyn sodium. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Glucocorticoids
retrieved_docs.content: As with outpatient therapy, clinicians should administer glucocorticoids promptly to children with moderate-to-severe asthma exacerbations, as their early administration reduces hospital admission rates. Oral and IV glucocorticoids have equivalent effects when given in comparable doses. [46] OCS are equally efficacious and preferred due to their less invasive nature. Dexamethasone, at 0.6 mg/kg/d for 2 days, is often preferred in the emergency department due to its long half-life and equivalent efficacy to prednisone. [47] [48] When administering prednisone or prednisolone, the dosage is 1 to 2 mg/kg/d with a maximum dosage of 20 mg/d in children aged 0 to 2, 30 mg/d in children aged 3 to 5, and 40 mg/d in children aged 6 to 11 for 3 to 5 days. A shorter course and lower dose of OCS are equally as effective as a higher dose and longer course. IV steroids are necessary for patients with impending or actual respiratory arrest or those who are intolerant of oral glucocorticoids.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma (Nursing) -- Nursing Management -- Management of Acute Exacerbations
retrieved_docs.content: Oral and IV steroids have been demonstrated to have equivalent potency in treating acute asthma exacerbations. Patients should be given prednisolone PO or methylprednisolone IV 1 to 2 mg/kg/day or dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO or IV depending on their level of respiratory distress and ability to swallow. Dexamethasone has been shown non-inferior to a short course of prednisone or prednisolone for an acute exacerbation. [7]

retrieved_docs.content: Step 6: Daily high-dose ICS-LABA + oral systemic corticosteroid and PRN SABA. The alternative regimen is daily high-dose ICS + montelukast + oral systemic corticosteroid and PRN SABA.

retrieved_docs.title: Status Asthmaticus -- Treatment / Management -- Indications for Hospitalization and Intensive Care
retrieved_docs.content: The ultimate disposition depends on both clinical and psychosocial factors. Hospitalization may be considered for patients with poor access to outpatient follow-up who might otherwise be candidates for discharge.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Evaluation and Treatment of Asthma in Children -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prehospital management of pediatric asthma varies across EMS agencies, underscoring the need for standardized, evidence-based protocols and continued research in this area. [36] [37] Incorporating corticosteroid administration into prehospital protocols, regardless of asthma severity, has been associated with reduced emergency department and hospital length of stay, shorter overall duration of care, and improved outcomes, including lower rates of ICU admission for status asthmaticus. Following protocol updates, prehospital use of ipratropium bromide in children with asthma increased. This greater utilization may have contributed to improved clinical prognosis and hospital outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Cromolyn Sodium -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Even though these are rare, effective communication between healthcare professionals is crucial in monitoring and managing potential adverse events or hypersensitivity reactions. Nurses and pharmacists must be vigilant in recognizing any adverse events and collaborate with the clinical team to manage them. In a severe reaction, the healthcare team must quickly withdraw cromolyn, administer emergency treatments like epinephrine, oxygen, antihistamines, and corticosteroids, and ensure continuous patient monitoring. A pharmacist should review the patient’s medication records to avoid future complications, while the intensivist may be consulted for hospital observation. After a hypersensitivity event, clinicians must discuss alternative therapies with the patient. This interprofessional coordination enhances patient safety and team performance in cromolyn sodium management for asthma, allergic rhinitis, and mastocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In the context of the COVID-19 pandemic, it is essential to remember that CDC has recognized asthma as a risk factor for severe COVID-19. [62] In patients with severe asthma using biologic therapy, the vaccine and the biologics should not be administered on the same day. [63] Optimal use of pharmacotherapy according to guidelines, use of a step-wise approach, and assessment of compliance and control at each visit can significantly reduce the morbidity, mortality, emergency room visits, and healthcare costs associated with asthma. Clinicians should also emphasize using preferred therapy at each step to optimize pharmacotherapy and minimize ADR. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Magnesium sulfate
retrieved_docs.content: A Cochrane Database review in 2014 concluded that a single infusion of intravenous MgSO 4 for patients not responding to conventional therapy results in improved lung functions and fewer hospital admissions. [37] However, in a recent systematic review, the comparison of the same studies, eliminating those involving children and those containing only abstracts, revealed conflicting results. The review examined the effects of intravenous and nebulized MgSO 4 . Although 3 out of 9 studies addressing treatment with intravenous MgSO 4 found a significant effect on lung function compared to placebo, these results are not statistically significant. [38] Only 2 of the 8 studies investigating hospital admission rates reveal a significant effect of MgSO 4 . [38] Conversely, 6 of the 9 studies investigating treatment with nebulized MgSO 4 compared to placebo reveal a favorable effect on the FEV 1 and peak expiratory flow rate. [38] These results somewhat contradict the Cochrane Database review conducted in 2014, which evaluated the same studies. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 36
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network of inhaled, intranasal, and systemic glucocorticoid therapies used for obstructive airway diseases and related conditions. Adrenergic inhalants combined with corticosteroids (excluding anticholinergics) form a therapeutic class that includes fixed‑dose products such as albuterol + beclomethasone, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone (Momesalic), and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate; budesonide, Momesalic, and fluticasone furoate are the corticosteroid components of these combinations. The same corticosteroids—budesonide, Momesalic, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate—appear in topical nasal preparations and in the class of glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease, which also encompasses systemic glucocorticoid derivatives such as hydrocortisone sodium succinate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and the child agent triamcinolone diacetate. These agents are employed with long‑acting β‑agonists (formoterol, vilanterol) or short‑acting β‑agonists (albuterol) to treat asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and allergic rhinitis. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, administered intravenously, is additionally indicated for acute bronchial or asthmatic exacerbations and for circulatory collapse, illustrating the therapeutic continuum from topical anti‑inflammatory inhalation to systemic emergency management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: Momesalic (mometasone), ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate function as common corticosteroid components across multiple delivery routes—combined adrenergic inhalants, nasal sprays, and standalone inhaled glucocorticoids—while the adrenergic‑corticosteroid combinations pair these steroids with long‑acting β2‑agonists such as formoterol, albuterol, and vilanterol to enhance bronchodilation. The inclusion of dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate as child entities further broadens therapeutic options for managing obstructive airway disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 621
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatories, Inhaled Corticosteroids comprise several specific agents, including beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát, and flutikason propionát, each classified as a child of this drug class. All three inhaled corticosteroids, together with triamcinolonacetonid, have been reported to may prevent the condition referred to as الرَبْو, whereas prednison may be used to treat الرَبْو. In contrast, kromoglykát dvojsodný (disodium cromoglycate) is contraindicated in patients with الرَبْو. Environmental exposure to znečištění ovzduší (air pollution) is related to the development or exacerbation of الرَبْو. Clinically, الرَبْو is associated with increased risk of وفاة حديث الولادة (newborn death) and is linked to مرض الانسداد الرئوي المزمن (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). Additionally, الرَبْو shares clinical similarity with asthma, suggesting overlapping pathophysiology or symptomatology. INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated not only with الرَبْو but also with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is further associated with وفاة حديث الولادة, indicating that infants born preterm and of low birth weight may face a cascade of adverse outcomes that include growth restriction and increased neonatal mortality. Together, these interconnections highlight the importance of preventive inhaled corticosteroid therapy, careful selection of contraindicated agents, and mitigation of environmental risk factors to improve respiratory health and reduce mortality in vulnerable neonatal populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 292
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” comprises several fixed‑dose inhaler combinations used for airway diseases, including albuterol + beclomethasone, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone (commercially Momésalic) and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate. In these products the corticosteroid components are beclomethasone, budesonide, mometasone (Momésalic) and fluticasone furoate. Corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use share the same agents—flutikason (fluticasone), Momésalic, budesonid (budesonide), ciclesonide, flunisolide and fluticasone furoate—demonstrating their dual role in intranasal therapy for allergic rhinitis and in inhaled therapy for obstructive airway disease. Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease include the above inhaled corticosteroids as well as prednisolone acetate (a child subclass), prednisolone sodium succinate and triamcinolone diacetate. All of these glucocorticoid inhalants act by reducing airway inflammation, thereby improving airflow and symptom control in patients with asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and related obstructive airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 407
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” comprises several fixed‑dose combinations used for airway disease, including albuterol + beclomethasone drug combinations, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone, and the vilanterol + fluticasone furoate combination. Within the budesonide / formoterol product, the corticosteroid component budesonid is contained, while fluticasone furoate is a constituent of the vilanterol + fluticasone furoate formulation, reflecting a close pharmacologic relationship between these agents. Corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use share many of the same inhaled glucocorticoids, listing budesonid, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate as member drugs. Likewise, the category “Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease” includes the identical set of agents—budesonid, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate—demonstrating overlap between nasal and inhaled delivery routes for these anti‑inflammatory medications. This inhalant class also has subordinate (child) products such as hydrocortisone sodium succinate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate, which represent related glucocorticoid salts used in various formulations. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, one of the child glucocorticoids, may be indicated for treatment in specific clinical contexts described by the Arabic terms “حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة” (a wet or edematous state) and “الانهيار في الدورة الدموية” (circulatory collapse), indicating its utility in managing severe fluid‑related or hemodynamic emergencies. Together, these relationships illustrate how multiple corticosteroid agents are organized across nasal and inhaled therapeutic categories, how they combine with adrenergic bronchodilators in fixed‑dose products, and how certain glucocorticoid salts are employed in critical care scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a therapeutic class that includes ciclesonide and flunisolide as member agents. Within this class, several related glucocorticoid compounds are identified as child entities, namely dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These inhaled glucocorticoids are employed to reduce inflammation and improve airflow in patients with obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. In addition to their inhaled applications, dexamethasone acetate has been recognized for its potential role in the management of severe systemic inflammation, exemplified by its use in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of glucocorticoid therapy, ranging from targeted inhalation for airway disease to broader systemic anti‑inflammatory interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 1051
retrieved_docs.content: The class “Anti‑inflammatories, Inhaled Corticosteroids” includes beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát, and flutikason propionát as specific child agents. In addition, dexamethasonisonikotinát and triamcinolonacetonid are inhaled corticosteroids that may prevent الرَبْو (asthma). These inhaled corticosteroids are key anti‑inflammatory medications used to prevent or control asthma, reducing airway inflammation and related symptoms. Cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with الرَبْو, indicating that patients undergoing cerclage may have an increased incidence or consideration of asthma. الرَبْو is also clinically similar to itself and is related to environmental factors such as znečištění ovzduší (air pollution). Moreover, الرَبْو has a relationship with مرض نفسي جسدي (psychosomatic disease), suggesting that psychological factors can influence asthma manifestation. Similarly, ضيق النَّفَس (dyspnea) is linked to مرض نفسي جسدي, highlighting the interplay between mental health and respiratory symptoms. kromoglykát dvojsodný (cromolyn sodium) is listed as contraindicated with الرَبْو in certain contexts, although it is generally used as a mast‑cell stabilizer for asthma prophylaxis; this apparent contradiction reflects that the drug may be unsuitable for patients with specific asthma phenotypes or comorbidities. Overall, inhaled corticosteroids such as beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát, flutikason propionát, dexamethasonisonikotinát, and triamcinolonacetonid are central to preventing and managing الرَبْو, a disease influenced by air pollution, associated with psychosomatic factors, and linked to procedures like cerclage. Careful assessment of contraindications, such as the use of kromoglykát dvojsodný in certain asthma patients, is essential for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 445
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excluding anticholinergics, constitute a therapeutic class that includes several fixed‑dose combinations such as albuterol + beclomethasone, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone, and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate. Within the formoterol / mometasone combination, the corticosteroid Momesalic is the component that provides the anti‑inflammatory effect. Fluticasone furoate, which is a component of the vilanterol + fluticasone furoate combination, is also classified as a corticosteroid nasal preparation for topical use and as a glucocorticoid inhalant for obstructive airway disease. Accordingly, the nasal preparation class and the inhalant glucocorticoid class share several members, including Momesalic, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate. The glucocorticoid inhalant category further encompasses related steroid agents that are listed as “children” of this class: dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific corticosteroid and adrenergic agents are organized into combination products and therapeutic groups used for airway inflammation, allergic rhinitis, and obstructive lung diseases, supporting integrated prescribing and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 418
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” comprises fixed‑dose inhalers that pair a β‑adrenergic bronchodilator with an inhaled corticosteroid. Representative products include albuterol‑beclomethasone, budesonide/formoterol, formoterol/mometasone, and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate; within each combination the corticosteroid component is budesonide, mometasone, or fluticasone furoate, respectively. The same corticosteroids—budesonide (listed as budesonid), mometasone (Momesalic), ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate—are also classified under “Corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use,” reflecting their dual utility as nasal sprays for upper‑airway inflammation. All of these agents belong to the broader category “Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease,” a class of anti‑inflammatory medications used to treat asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. This category includes Momesalic, budesonid, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate, and it has child entities representing related steroid formulations such as dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate (the latter being a specific glucocorticoid inhalant within the same therapeutic group). Collectively, these relationships illustrate that specific corticosteroids serve multiple therapeutic roles: as standalone nasal preparations, as components of β‑adrenergic combination inhalers, and as primary glucocorticoid inhalants for lower‑airway obstructive diseases, providing clinicians with versatile options for managing both upper and lower airway inflammation.

In addition to adrenergic agents, anti‑inflammatory inhaled corticosteroids constitute another major subclass of drugs for obstructive airway diseases. The anti‑inflammatory inhaled corticosteroid group includes specific agents such as beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate (also listed as beklomethason dipropionát) and flutikason propionát, each representing a child concept within the broader inhaled corticosteroid category. Overall, the therapeutic landscape for obstructive airway diseases integrates both adrenergic bronchodilators—delivered either by inhalation or systemically—and inhaled corticosteroid anti‑inflammatories. Recognizing the

retrieved_docs.content: The category Anti‑inflammatories, Inhaled Corticosteroids comprises agents such as beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát and flutikason propionát, which are inhaled corticosteroids used in respiratory care. These inhaled corticosteroids, together with dexamethasone‑isonicotinate, have been reported to may prevent the condition referred to as الرَبْو. Conversely, the mast‑cell stabilizer kromoglykát dvojsodný (cromolyn sodium) is contraindicated in patients with الرَبْو. Clinically, الرَبْو is associated with several obstetric and neonatal factors: it is linked to the use of the cerclage procedure, to infant preterm low birth weight, and to neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة). The condition is also considered clinically similar to asthma and is related to environmental exposure such as air pollution (znečištění ovzduší). Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology includes الرَبْو as a concept and also includes neonatal death as a subset concept. Additionally, infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature itself is associated with neonatal death, highlighting a cascade of adverse outcomes in the perinatal period.

retrieved_docs.content: Prematurity (خديج) is clinically associated with the condition الرَبْو and also with cesarean delivery (الولادة القيصرية); in turn, premature birth can lead to pulmonary maturation disorder syndrome (متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة). Infant preterm low birth weight is linked to both الرَبْو and cesarean delivery, highlighting the interrelationship between low birth weight, prematurity, and delivery method. Optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) shows clinical associations with both الرَبْو and cesarean delivery, suggesting that decisions around pregnancy termination may influence or be influenced by these obstetric factors. Neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) is clinically associated with الرَبْو, underscoring the severe outcomes that can accompany this condition. Environmental air pollution (znečištění ovzduší) is related to الرَبْو, indicating an external risk factor that may exacerbate the condition. Asthma is clinically similar to الرَبْو, reflecting overlapping respiratory symptomatology. Cromolyn sodium (kromoglykát dvojsodný) is contraindicated in patients with الرَبْو, emphasizing the need to avoid this medication in such cases. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 487
retrieved_docs.content: In pediatric and obstetric medicine, asthma (الربو) is interconnected with a range of perinatal, pharmacologic, and environmental factors. Infants born preterm with low birth weight are clinically associated with a higher risk of developing asthma and with short stature, and both conditions are linked to increased rates of neonatal death. The obstetric procedure cerclage is also clinically associated with later asthma, highlighting a possible connection between cervical insufficiency interventions and respiratory outcomes in offspring. Air‑pollution exposure (znečištění ovzduší) is recognized as an environmental contributor that relates to asthma incidence. Pharmacologically, the class “Anti‑inflammatories, Inhaled Corticosteroids” includes several agents that may prevent asthma, namely beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát, flutikason propionát, dexamethasonisonikotinát, and triamcinolonacetonid. These inhaled corticosteroids are considered preventive therapies for asthma in children. In contrast, the mast‑cell stabilizer cromolyn sodium (kromoglykát dvojsodný) is contraindicated in patients with asthma, reflecting a disease‑specific restriction on its use. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology (PAET) catalogues asthma alongside serious neonatal adverse events such as umbilical‑cord prolapse (تدلّي الحبل السري) and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة), underscoring the clinical relevance of monitoring these outcomes together. Collectively, these relationships emphasize the importance of integrated care that addresses perinatal risk factors, environmental exposures, and appropriate pharmacologic prevention to improve respiratory health and reduce adverse neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma is considered clinically similar to الرَبْو, indicating that the two conditions share comparable clinical features. The condition الرَبْو is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة, suggesting that patients with الرَبْو have an increased risk of newborn death. Cromoglykát dvojsodný (cromoglycate disodium) is contraindicated in individuals who have الرَبْو, meaning that this mast‑cell stabilizer should not be used in the presence of that condition. Conversely, triamcinolonacetonid (triamcinolone acetonide) may prevent the development or progression of الرَبْو, highlighting its potential therapeutic role. Additionally, znečištění ovzduší (air pollution) is related to الرَبْو, indicating that exposure to polluted air may contribute to the occurrence or worsening of this condition. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of recognizing clinical similarities between asthma and الرَبْو, monitoring for serious outcomes such as newborn death, selecting appropriate medications, and addressing environmental risk factors to improve patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1304
retrieved_docs.content: The condition denoted by the Arabic term “الرَبْو” is closely linked to respiratory disease. It is clinically similar to asthma and to chronic asthmatic bronchitis, indicating overlapping symptomatology and pathophysiology. The obstetric procedure cerclage is clinically associated with الرَبْو, suggesting that the condition may be encountered in patients undergoing this intervention. Environmental exposure, specifically air pollution (Czech: znečištění ovzduší), is related to the development or exacerbation of الرَبْو. Pharmacologically, inhaled corticosteroids—classified under anti‑inflammatories, inhaled corticosteroids—such as beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, its Czech synonym beklomethason dipropionát, and flutikason propionát (fluticasone propionate) may prevent the occurrence or worsening of الرَبْو. In contrast, the mast‑cell stabilizer cromolyn sodium (Czech: kromoglykát dvojsodný) is contraindicated in patients with الرَبْو, indicating that its use could be harmful. This integrated knowledge highlights the respiratory nature of الرَبْو, its environmental risk factors, and the importance of selecting appropriate inhaled corticosteroid therapy while avoiding contraindicated agents for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 446
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a therapeutic class of inhaled glucocorticoid medications used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. This class is positioned as a subclass of triamcinolonacetonid, indicating a hierarchical relationship to the glucocorticoid triamcinolone acetonide. Within the Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease category, several specific agents are identified as child compounds: prednison, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These individual glucocorticoid formulations can be delivered by inhalation to reduce airway inflammation and improve respiratory function in patients with obstructive airway disease.
